summary.intent: Remove the legacy Session table and all hook handler infrastructure, leaving only the indexer/poll-loop pipeline and the serve endpoint as the active data path.
summary.outcome: Deleted handler.ts and index.ts entirely; dropped Session, Event, AgentParent, PendingToolUse, and all v1 Checkpoint tables from the schema; fixed a regression where gitUserName disappeared from search results by reading it from git config at server startup instead of a DB join; pushed the new schema to the live DB after manually dropping FTS5 virtual tables that blocked prisma db push.

repo: The Session table was purely a cross-invocation scratchpad for the hook handler — all its data (cwd, prompt, summary, keywords) was already redundantly stored in the Event table's data JSON column.
gitUser (name/email) is read from git config at server startup and is available as a module-level variable in server.ts — no DB join needed to enrich search results.
The gossamer DB contains FTS5 virtual tables that prisma db push cannot drop; they must be manually dropped with SQLite DROP TABLE before prisma can proceed.
The old handler.js process was not killed by pkill on the first attempt, causing the new server to appear unresponsive — the stale process was still serving on port 3000.

code.finding: db.session.findUnique was used to look up cwd for the 30s auto-link check; replaced by JSON.parse(recentEnd.data).cwd since the full HookInput is already in the Event table.
applyEvent deletes state.sessions[session_id] on SessionEnd, meaning ended sessions were already removed from in-memory state but writeStateToDb never pruned corresponding DB rows.
summary and keywords were written as columns on the UserPromptSubmit Event row inside writeEventLog, making the Session table copies fully redundant.
LEFT JOIN Session was used solely to enrich results with gitUserName/gitUserEmail; removed and replaced by server-side injection from git config.

gitUser is read from git config at server startup; spreading gitUser.name and gitUser.email onto each search result in the /api/search handler restores the gitUserName field without any DB dependency.

workflow: When prisma db push is blocked by virtual tables (FTS5), manually drop them via sqlite3 CLI before re-running the push.
After killing a server process, verify the port is actually free before starting a replacement — stale processes can survive pkill.
Running npm run build after each edit is an effective fast feedback loop for catching type errors before touching the running server.

friction: prisma db push failed because it cannot drop SQLite FTS5 virtual tables — required a manual sqlite3 DROP TABLE workaround.
pkill did not terminate the old handler.js process on the first attempt, leaving a stale server on port 3000 that masked the new server's behavior.
gitUserName regression was introduced by removing the Session LEFT JOIN without immediately providing a replacement source, requiring a separate fix pass.
open_items: The live DB still had the old tables at session end — db push with --accept-data-loss was needed; any environments not yet pushed will need the same migration.
FTS tables were dropped and will be rebuilt by the server on startup — verify rebuild completes correctly and search quality is unchanged.
search.ts no longer returns gitUserName/gitUserEmail from a per-session source; if per-session git identity ever differs from the server-level git config, results will be wrong.

The schema no longer has the old v1 Checkpoint/CheckpointSession/CheckpointMessage tables — confirm no other consumers (e.g., external scripts, reporting tools) depend on them before treating the drop as final.
